Idiom (Fon): Yónu tɔn non hú asinzen à
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed descriptive clause with possessed noun phrase (body-part construction) used metaphorically.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person who travels constantly and rarely stays settled in one place.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Someone who moves frequently and is rarely at home.
Usage Contexts: Daily conversation, migration, business travel, social commentary.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: In Fon households ants are everywhere; someone whose butt 'does not kill ants' never sits long enough for ants to bite.
First Example Sentence: Sètónu yónu tɔn non hú asinzen à; é yi Cotonou bó trɔ̀ wá Abomey gbè.
Second Example Sentence: Kòfí dɔ̀ gã, yónu tɔn non hú asinzen à, é kpó xwé ɖevoɖevo.
Third Example Sentence: Amɛ lɛ́ sọ̀ nɛ́ yónu tɔn non hú asinzen à elabɔ́ é mè kpɔ̀ ɖokpó o.

Idiom (Fon): Sor wè t’afor xo tɔn me á
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To have an endless appetite or behave as if one can never be satisfied.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: To always be hungry or want more food.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The horse symbolizes strong appetite and energy in West African folklore.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Fon): [sɔ́ɾ wɛ̀ t‿à.fɔ́ɾ xɔ́ tɔ̃ mɛ́ á]
First Example Sentence: Tòsí dù nú hùnhùn gudo bó é gblɔ̀ nɛ́ é hun; mí bi nɛ́ sor wè t’afor xo tɔn me á?
Second Example Sentence: É dù nú we ji bó dù àgbàdo ji sín; mí hùn nɛ́ sor wè t’afor xo tɔn me á?
Third Example Sentence: Nú vi yìí dù nú gã, nɔnɔ́ tɔn gblɔ̀ nɛ́ sor wè t’afor xo tɔn me á.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verb phrase with comparative predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To sleep lightly and wake easily.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: To sleep only briefly or lightly.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Cats are known in Fon homes for short, alert sleep cycles.
First Example Sentence: Vi ɖe lɛ́ dor awíí mlon; adán ɖokpó ko é fón.
Second Example Sentence: Nye dor awíí mlon zã vɔ́vɔ́ elabɔ́ xóɖóɖó kpó mí.
Third Example Sentence: Nɔ̀nù tɔn dor awíí mlon bó é se gbe xwé blibó.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed declarative clause with object noun phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To create unnecessary trouble for oneself.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Refers to the absurd image of sewing and wearing an iron shirt.
First Example Sentence: Nú mìɖé xɔ̀ xó lɛ́ lɔ́, é tor ganwu do kɔ̀.
Second Example Sentence: Mà kpá xó yìí o; nù yìí nyɛ́ é tor ganwu do kɔ̀.
Third Example Sentence: Tàví kpé ɖevo ji; mí gblɔ̀ nɛ́ é tor ganwu do kɔ̀.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Something flashy but temporary or unreliable.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Something that will not last long.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Expression referring to decorations that fade after a few days.
First Example Sentence: Xó sɔ́sɔ́ yìí nyɛ́ azan àton gbè tchô.
Second Example Sentence: Nú alɔ́ gã mè kpó nù o, mí gblɔ̀ nɛ́ azan àton gbè tchô.
Third Example Sentence: Alɔ́ tɔn nyɛ́ azan àton gbè tchô; zǎn atọ̀n vɔ́ bó é gbó.

Idiom (Fon): É goxò có gi wá honto
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To already be satisfied or secure before scarcity arrives.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: To be financially secure and comfortable.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Refers to maize dumplings, a staple in Fon cuisine.
First Example Sentence: Nú wémɛ́ lɛ́ bi alɔ̀ nú ẹ, é gblɔ̀ nɛ́ é goxò có gi wá honto.
Second Example Sentence: Kpɔ́njí ɖé o; é goxò có gi wá honto.
Third Example Sentence: Amɛ lɛ́ sọ̀ nɛ́ é goxò có gi wá honto elabɔ́ é mè hùn nù o.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone resilient who remains strong under pressure.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A person who performs well despite difficulty.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Based on soap resisting friction with a sponge during bathing.
First Example Sentence: Nú dɔ̀ xwéme lɛ́ dìn, Kòkú nyɛ́ adi é kpé tékan ji.
Second Example Sentence: Nɔnɔ́ yìí kpé gã; adi é kpé tékan ji.
Third Example Sentence: Nú amɛ ɖé to asɔ̀títọ́ mẹ̀ bó ko mà dù o, mí gblɔ̀ nɛ́ adi é kpé tékan ji.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase with object noun phrase.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from imagery of opening a closed container.
First Example Sentence: É kpó nuɖé nú mɛ̀ bó amɛ lɛ́ se xó yìí.
Second Example Sentence: Kòjò kpó nuɖé nú mɛ̀ gudo bó xó gbó.
Third Example Sentence: Nú amɛ ɖé kpó nuɖé nú mɛ̀, xó kpó kpó.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase with intensifying noun phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To have great influence or power.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: To be very powerful or influential.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agbè symbolizes authority in traditional Fon society.
First Example Sentence: Tàví yìí kpé agbè gã nú xwéme.
Second Example Sentence: Amɛ lɛ́ sọ̀ nɛ́ é kpé agbè gã.
Third Example Sentence: Nú alɔ́ tɔn gã, é kpé agbè gã.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed idiomatic clause with object complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To exaggerate or dramatize a story.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects oral storytelling traditions among Fon speakers.
First Example Sentence: É kpó xó do mɛ̀ bó amɛ lɛ́ ɖo àfọ̀.
Second Example Sentence: Nú é gblɔ̀ xó yìí, é kpó xó do mɛ̀.
Third Example Sentence: Amɛ lɛ́ hùn nɛ́ é kpó xó do mɛ̀ gã.
